preamble: The phonetic transformation from an alveolar trill /r/ to a voiced velar fricative /ɣ/ involves a shift in the place of articulation from the alveolar ridge to the velum, and a change in the manner of articulation from a trill, produced by the vibration of the tongue tip, to a fricative, characterized by turbulent airflow through a narrow constriction. This sound change is a form of backing and can be considered a type of lenition, where a more complex articulation (trill) is replaced by a simpler one (fricative).
phoneticEffects: Backing, Lenition (Trill to Fricative), Change in Place of Articulation (Alveolar to Velar), Change in Manner of Articulation (Trill to Fricative)

examples.note: The original pre-Masoretic Hebrew /r/ is posited to have evolved to /ɣ/ during the first millennium CE, although the exact intermediate steps (e.g., via /ɾ/) are debated.
Pre-Pakanic initial *r- frequently changed to /ɣ-/ in Proto-Pakanic, a shift also observed in many Angkuic languages.
Documented in certain Qiltu dialects of Southern Iraq.
Observed in the phonology of Arabic loanwords in Central Kurdish.

sources: SciSpace. (2016). A PRELIMINARY RECONSTRUCTION OF PROTO-PAKANIC.
Alshawi, W. (2015). A sociolinguistic study of a southern Iraqi dialect: fortition of the variants [j] and [ʧ]. University of Essex.
White Rose eTheses Online. (2015). An analysis of Arabic loanwords in spoken Central Kurdish.
